children.name: Talpa
children.description: True moles of Europe and Asia — subterranean insectivores with powerful, spade-like forelimbs for digging, a cylindrical, fur-covered body with no obvious neck, tiny vestigial eyes covered by skin or fused eyelids, and no external ear pinnae. The European mole can dig through 20 metres of soil per hour. Its fur lies in any direction, allowing the mole to reverse in its tunnel without resistance. Moles are solitary and aggressively territorial; males expand their tunnel systems dramatically in spring when searching for mates. The mole's blood contains a unique double-bonded haemoglobin that allows it to use CO₂-rich air in its burrow far more efficiently than surface mammals.

children.name: Talpa europaea
children.description: Almost entirely subterranean, surfacing mainly at dawn in early summer. Creates extensive tunnel systems up to 100 m long. Caches living but immobilised earthworms in chambers, sometimes storing hundreds.

children.name: Talpa caeca
children.description: Found in southern Europe and Turkey, distinguished by completely fused eyelids — the most visually reduced of the European moles. Inhabits meadows, scrubland, and cultivated land; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa occidentalis
children.description: Endemic to the Iberian Peninsula, occupying the Atlantic and Mediterranean zones of Spain and Portugal. Slightly smaller than the European mole with a more slender snout; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa romana
children.description: Restricted to southern Italy and parts of the western Balkans. Larger than Talpa europaea and with relatively broader forefeet; occupies pastures and woodland edges. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa stankovici
children.description: Occurs in the western Balkans, including North Macedonia and Albania. A medium-sized Talpa species associated with upland meadows and cultivated fields; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa levantis
children.description: Distributed across Turkey, Georgia, and adjacent parts of the Middle East. Adapted to a range of habitats from coastal lowlands to montane meadows; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa altaica
children.description: The easternmost Talpa, ranging from western Siberia through the Altai and into Kazakhstan. Can survive in seasonally frozen soils and constructs deep burrows to avoid permafrost; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa caucasica
children.description: Endemic to the Caucasus mountains of Georgia, Armenia, and adjacent Russia. Inhabits subalpine meadows at elevations up to 2,500 m; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Talpa davidiana
children.description: Named after the French missionary and naturalist Père Armand David, who collected the type specimen in China. Occurs in central China in montane habitats; IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Talpa talyschensis
children.description: Described from the humid Talysh Mountains of southern Azerbaijan and the adjacent Elburz foothills of northern Iran, near the southern Caspian coast. Distinguished from the Levant mole by molecular divergence and subtle cranial differences; a specialist of Hyrcanian deciduous forest soils. IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Talpa grodzinski
children.description: Described from the mountains of northeastern Turkey and the adjacent South Caucasus, sympatric with but molecularly distinct from the Caucasian mole (T. caucasica). Occupies subalpine meadows and mixed forest edges at elevations above 1,500 m; IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Condylura
children.description: The star-nosed mole — one of the most extraordinary mammals on Earth, found in wet, low-lying ground in eastern North America. Its nose bears 22 fleshy, pink tentacles arranged in a star pattern, containing over 25,000 minute sensory receptors (Eimer's organs) — the most sensitive touch organ of any mammal. The star-nosed mole is the world's fastest-eating mammal, able to identify and consume prey in as little as 120 milliseconds — faster than the human eye can follow. It is also semi-aquatic, swimming through streams and foraging under ice in winter using its extraordinary nasal sense.

children.name: Condylura cristata
children.description: The world's fastest-eating mammal. Its 22-tentacled nose contains more sensory nerve fibres than a human hand. Can detect, identify, and consume prey in 120 ms. Swims underwater and hunts beneath ice in winter.

children.name: Desmana
children.description: The Russian desman — an ancient, semi-aquatic insectivore related to moles but specialised for life in rivers and streams. With its long, flexible, trunk-like snout bearing Eimer's organs, webbed feet, and a flattened, scaly tail used as a rudder, the desman is one of the most bizarre mammals in Europe. It is now critically endangered — its range has contracted dramatically due to water pollution, river engineering, and mink predation. Fewer than 30,000 individuals may remain in the Don, Volga, and Ural river basins.

children.name: Desmana moschata
children.description: One of Europe's rarest and most ancient mammals. Now restricted to river systems in Russia and Kazakhstan. Produces strong musk; historically hunted for this secretion and its dense, waterproof fur.

children.name: Galemys
children.description: The Pyrenean desman — a small, semi-aquatic insectivore endemic to the mountain streams of the Pyrenees and Cantabrian Mountains of Spain, Portugal, and southern France. Related to the Russian desman but only distantly, the two desman genera separated approximately 30 million years ago. The Pyrenean desman forages in fast-flowing, oxygen-rich mountain streams, using its long, flexible snout to probe among rocks for invertebrates. It is endangered due to water abstraction, pollution, and the introduction of American mink and signal crayfish.

children.name: Galemys pyrenaicus
children.description: Endangered and declining. Found only in clean, fast-flowing mountain streams in the Pyrenees and Iberian Peninsula. Requires pristine water quality; a sensitive indicator of stream ecosystem health.

children.name: Scalopus
children.description: The eastern mole genus of North America — robust, fully fossorial moles with broad, naked, paddle-shaped forefeet and a naked, short tail. The single living species is the most widespread mole in North America, found from the Great Plains to the Atlantic coast.

children.name: Scalopus aquaticus
children.description: The most common mole in eastern North America, found from southern Ontario to Florida and west to the Great Plains. Creates shallow foraging tunnels and deeper winter burrows; feeds primarily on earthworms. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Scapanus
children.description: Western North American moles — larger and more powerfully built than eastern moles, occurring along the Pacific coast and Cascades. They inhabit moist soils and are significant ecosystem engineers in Pacific coastal forests and grasslands.

children.name: Scapanus townsendii
children.description: The largest North American mole, named after naturalist John Kirk Townsend. Restricted to the Willamette Valley and coastal Oregon and Washington; listed as Vulnerable due to its very limited range and agricultural land conversion.

children.name: Scapanus latimanus
children.description: Also known as the California mole, widespread in California and Baja California in a variety of habitats from coastal scrub to oak woodland. Tolerates drier soils than its congeners; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Scapanus orarius
children.description: Found in the coastal Pacific Northwest from British Columbia to northern California, preferring moist, loamy soils of forests and meadows. Medium-sized; often syntopic with Townsend's mole but occupies wetter microhabitats. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Parascalops
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the hairy-tailed mole of northeastern North America. Distinguished by its distinctive, hairy tail — unlike the naked tail of most other North American moles.

children.name: Parascalops breweri
children.description: Named after ornithologist Thomas Mayo Brewer. Found in well-drained sandy soils of northeastern North America, from the Appalachians to the Maritime Provinces. Its hairy tail distinguishes it from all other North American moles. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mogera
children.description: Japanese and East Asian moles — a genus of medium-sized, fully fossorial moles found throughout Japan, Korea, China, and adjacent areas. Several species are island endemics with restricted ranges. They inhabit forested hills, agricultural land, and scrub.

children.name: Mogera wogura
children.description: The largest Japanese mole, widely distributed across Honshu, Kyushu, and Shikoku. Occupies a broad range of habitats including forests, grasslands, and cultivated land; an important soil aerator in Japanese agriculture. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mogera imaizumii
children.description: Named after Japanese mammalogist Yoshinori Imaizumi. A small mole restricted to parts of Honshu, Japan; typically found in upland forests and meadows at moderate elevations. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mogera robusta
children.description: A large, robust mole from northeastern China, the Korean Peninsula, and the Russian Far East. Occupies agricultural fields and forest edges; one of the most heavily built moles in Asia. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mogera insularis
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan, found in lowland and montane forests across the island. A medium-sized mole with limited range; considered Near Threatened due to ongoing habitat loss from agricultural development.

children.name: Mogera tokudae
children.description: Named after Japanese zoologist Muneo Tokuda. A small, poorly known mole restricted to the Ryukyu Islands of Japan; listed as Endangered due to its extremely limited island range and ongoing habitat degradation.

children.name: Mogera etigo
children.description: Restricted to the alluvial plains and foothills of the Niigata region (historic province of Echigo) on the Sea of Japan coast of Honshu. Inhabits rice paddies and alluvial forest soils; IUCN Near Threatened due to its restricted range and ongoing agricultural intensification.

children.name: Mogera kobeae
children.description: Found in the Kinki region of western Honshu, centred around the Kobe and Osaka areas. Inhabits farmland and lowland forest edges in one of Japan's most densely populated regions; IUCN Least Concern but potentially threatened by ongoing urbanisation.

children.name: Mogera minor
children.description: A small Mogera species from southern parts of the Japanese archipelago, occupying lowland forests and grassland soils. Slightly smaller in body size than most congeners; distinguished from M. imaizumii by cranial morphology and geographic range. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Mogera uchidai
children.description: Endemic to Sado Island in the Sea of Japan, isolated from mainland populations for thousands of years. Listed as Vulnerable; sometimes placed in the monotypic genus Nesoscaptor to reflect its distinct evolutionary history. Agricultural habitat loss and island endemism make it one of Japan's most at-risk small mammals.

children.name: Mogera latouchei
children.description: Named after British naturalist J.D.D. La Touche, who collected extensively in Fujian and other southern Chinese provinces. Found from Fujian and Yunnan south into northern Vietnam and Myanmar, inhabiting montane forest soils at moderate elevations. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Euroscaptor
children.description: Asian moles distributed across South and East Asia, from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Japan. Distinguished from Talpa by various cranial and dental features; several species are montane specialists with restricted ranges.

children.name: Euroscaptor mizura
children.description: A high-altitude specialist restricted to montane forests and subalpine meadows of central and northern Honshu, Japan, above 1,000 m. One of the smallest moles in Asia; IUCN Vulnerable due to its narrow elevational and geographic range.

children.name: Euroscaptor klossi
children.description: Named after British naturalist Cecil Boden Kloss. Found in montane forests of the Malay Peninsula and northern Sumatra; a poorly known species associated with highland habitats above 1,000 m. IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Euroscaptor longirostris
children.description: Distributed in southern China and northern Vietnam, characterised by an unusually elongated snout compared to related species. Inhabits montane forests; IUCN Least Concern but data remain limited.

children.name: Euroscaptor parvidens
children.description: A small mole from Vietnam and adjacent southern China, named for its reduced dentition. Lives in montane and submontane forests; one of several poorly studied Southeast Asian Euroscaptor species. IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Euroscaptor grandis
children.description: The largest member of the genus Euroscaptor, found in subtropical and temperate montane forests of Yunnan and Sichuan in southwestern China. Its greater body size and robust forelimbs allow it to excavate harder soils than smaller congeners; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Euroscaptor subanura
children.description: Described from hilly terrain of Anhui province in eastern China, distinguished from the long-nosed mole (E. longirostris) by molecular markers and a relatively shorter tail. Inhabits mixed forest and farmland soils in the Yangtze River basin; IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Euroscaptor malayanus
children.description: The southernmost-ranging Euroscaptor, restricted to the Cameron Highlands and other montane areas of the Malay Peninsula above 1,000 m. Inhabits the thick, humus-rich soils of montane oak and laurel forests; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Euroscaptor orlovi
children.description: Described from the Hoang Lien Son Mountains of northern Vietnam, where it inhabits montane forest soils above 1,500 m. Differentiated from other Southeast Asian Euroscaptor species by molecular systematics and cranial morphology; IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Euroscaptor kuznetsovi
children.description: Recognised through molecular systematic studies of Euroscaptor populations in southern China and adjacent Vietnam. Distinguished from the long-nosed mole (E. longirostris) and greater Chinese mole (E. grandis) by genetic divergence and subtle skull morphology; inhabits montane forest soils in Yunnan. IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Urotrichus
children.description: Japanese shrew moles — small, surface-active moles of Japan that represent an intermediate between true moles and shrew moles. They forage both above and below ground and retain functional, though small, eyes.

children.name: Urotrichus talpoides
children.description: A small, partly surface-active insectivore found in moist forests of Honshu, Shikoku, and Kyushu, Japan. Forages in leaf litter as well as shallow burrows; has small but functional eyes. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Dymecodon
children.description: A monotypic shrew mole genus endemic to Japan, distinguished by its elongated tail relative to other Japanese moles. Represents one of the most ancient lineages within the family.

children.name: Dymecodon pilirostris
children.description: Found in montane forests of Honshu, Japan; has a long, hairy snout and relatively long tail. Forages in moist soil and leaf litter; less fossorial than true moles. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Neurotrichus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the American shrew mole — the smallest talpid in North America and the sole member of its tribe Neurotrichini. It retains fully functional eyes and is highly surface-active compared to fossorial moles.

children.name: Neurotrichus gibbsii
children.description: Named after American geologist and naturalist George Gibbs. The smallest talpid in North America, found in moist forest and riparian habitats along the Pacific coast from British Columbia to California. Active above ground and swims readily. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Uropsilus
children.description: Chinese shrew moles — primitive, shrew-like talpids from montane forests of southwestern China and adjacent Myanmar. They retain long tails, small functional eyes, and are largely surface-active, representing an early branch of the talpid family tree.

children.name: Uropsilus soricipes
children.description: Found in montane forests of Sichuan and Yunnan, China, at elevations above 2,000 m. Superficially resembles a shrew; forages in leaf litter and under logs. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Uropsilus gracilis
children.description: A slender, gracile species from the mountains of Yunnan, China, and northern Myanmar. Closely related to U. soricipes but slightly smaller and with a more gracile skull; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Uropsilus andersoni
children.description: Named after Scottish zoologist John Anderson, who extensively studied the mammals of Yunnan. Occurs in cloud forests of southwestern China; one of the rarest and least-known talpids. IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Uropsilus investigator
children.description: Found in the Qinling and Daba Mountains of Shaanxi and Sichuan, China, at elevations above 2,000 m. The largest Uropsilus species; inhabits moist conifer and mixed forests, foraging in deep leaf litter and humus-rich soil for invertebrates. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Uropsilus atronates
children.description: Described from montane forests of Sichuan and Yunnan, China, distinguished by darker pigmentation on the dorsal nape. A poorly known shrew mole of old-growth mountain forest above 2,500 m; IUCN Data Deficient.

children.name: Scaptonyx
children.description: A monotypic Asian genus containing the long-tailed mole — an intermediate form between shrew moles and true moles, with a longer tail than most fossorial talpids. Found in montane southwestern China and adjacent areas.

children.name: Scaptonyx fusicaudus
children.description: Found in montane forests of southwestern China, Myanmar, and Vietnam at elevations up to 3,700 m. Has a distinctly elongated, fleshy tail unusual among talpids; moderately fossorial. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Scaptochirus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the short-faced mole of northern China — notable for its distinctively compressed, broad skull and very short rostrum compared to other talpids.

children.name: Scaptochirus moschatus
children.description: Endemic to the agricultural plains and loess hills of northern China, particularly Hebei and Shanxi provinces. Its extremely broad, flat skull is unique among moles; produces musk secretions. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Parascaptor
children.description: A monotypic genus from South and Southeast Asia containing the white-tailed mole — named for the pale tip of its tail. Formerly placed in Talpa or Mogera; now recognised as a distinct lineage.

children.name: Parascaptor leucura
children.description: Found in northeastern India, Myanmar, and Yunnan, China, inhabiting montane forests and grasslands. Its pale-tipped tail is distinctive; a fossorial species that feeds on invertebrates in moist hill soils. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Scapanulus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Owen's mole — found in the high mountains of central China. Scapanulus occupies an intermediate position in the talpid family tree, sharing features with both shrew moles and true moles; it has functional but reduced eyes and broader forelimbs than shrew moles, but is less completely fossorial than Talpa or Euroscaptor.

children.name: Scapanulus oweni
children.description: The sole species of genus Scapanulus, inhabiting the Qinling, Min, and Daba mountain ranges of Shaanxi, Gansu, and Sichuan, China, at elevations from 2,000 to 3,500 m. A morphological intermediate between shrew moles and true moles with semi-fossorial habits; IUCN Least Concern.
